publications.editedComment: Uh—this selector is less a dropdown than a whole connection desk hiding behind one server button. It lets the user choose separate default Art and Text servers, save OpenAI and Anthropic keys, and maintain private local ComfyUI, A1111, or Ollama endpoints. New local records start with type-specific endpoint and health defaults; editing exposes the advanced fields, and deleting is explicit. Provider-key forms and local-server forms remain separate, while status messages report success or failure instead of pretending every write landed. The dialog also cleans URLs and closes its temporary add/edit state when dismissed. Busy, yes, but the responsibilities are visible: choose defaults, store credentials, and manage endpoints—not run a generation itself.

This connection panel keeps routing choices and credentials in one accountable place. Separate selectors choose the default Art and Text servers, with the system/mana route available as the null choice. OpenAI and Anthropic keys are saved through dedicated forms, while local ComfyUI, A1111, and Ollama records receive their own URL, access mode, endpoint, health path, label, and model controls. Existing local servers can be edited or deleted; new records receive sensible type defaults without obscuring the fields. Status notes surface write outcomes, and closing the dialog cancels temporary editing state. The butterfly approves of a tool that prepares several flight paths without claiming to fly them itself.

Coffee's still warm. The left rail loads direct-message conversations, shows each peer, last message, and unread count, and opens a thread through the Conversation store. The right side renders messages by sender alignment, keeps the composer disabled while empty or sending, and reports the store's last error below it. A successful send clears the draft and scrolls to the newest message; changes in message count do the same. On mount, the page loads the conversation list and may open the numeric `?c=` thread from the route. No polling, friend search, or bot chat lives here. It is a focused two-pane client for the store's existing threaded-message state.

A conversation exists here as two coordinated lists: summaries on the left and the selected thread on the right. The Component asks `conversationStore` to load both, derives the peer from each summary's first participant, and compares message sender ids with the active user's id to place each bubble. Sending is conditional on an active thread and a nonblank draft; success empties the field and moves the scroll container to its bottom after the DOM update. The route may nominate a conversation through `?c=`, but the store remains the owner of messages, unread counts, sending state, and errors. The interface does not create relationships—it renders the traces already made between two people.

Two faces, one recovery page. Without a query token, it asks only for an email and calls `account.requestPasswordReset`. With `?token=...`, it replaces that form with new-password and confirmation fields and calls `account.resetPassword` using the route token. The reset button remains disabled until the password has at least eight characters and both entries match; `onReset` also guards the mismatch and writes a local error. Both store results supply the success flag and visible message. A successful token reset reveals the sign-in link. The Component does not inspect accounts or validate tokens locally. It presents the request, consumes the answer, and keeps the two recovery stages from staring at each other.

Punch up the split: no token means 'send me a link'; a token means 'set the new password.' The first form sends the entered email to `requestPasswordReset`. The second requires eight characters and an exact confirmation match before calling `resetPassword(token, next)`. Both operations mirror the Account store's `{ success, message }` into one feedback panel, and only a successful token reset adds the route back to sign-in. Loading state disables the active submit button and swaps in a spinner. There is no mystery account lookup or client-side token approval hiding here—the route selects the mode, the store performs the recovery work, and this Component keeps the user from submitting an obviously mismatched pair.

Four doors, and the URL gets first pick. `/wonderlab`, `/memory`, `/screenfx`, and `/animation-manager` each map directly to their own tab, so remembered dashboard state cannot pull an explicit route into a different room. Only when the path is not one of those does the Component consult `navStore.getDashboardTab('wonder')`; an invalid remembered value falls back to WonderLab. The template then mounts exactly one large surface: Memory Dungeon, the WonderLab router plus museum, Screen FX, or Animation Manager. An impossible tab receives a warning instead of a blank mirror. This manager owns routing and composition only—the child rooms own their actual behavior.

Routing puzzle solved in two moves. First, four explicit paths map authoritatively to Memory Dungeon, WonderLab, Screen FX, or Animation Manager. Second, every other embedding may reuse the remembered `wonder` dashboard tab, but only if its value appears in the four-item valid-tab set; otherwise the fallback is WonderLab. The template renders the matching child surface and gives the central WonderLab route both its selection router and `lab-interact`. Unknown values expose their name in a warning branch. No child state is coordinated here. The Component is the switchboard: route beats memory, validated memory beats fallback, and exactly one destination receives the available height.

The museum begins with a broad catalog, then narrows its spotlight. Search, folder, status, sort, review-coverage, discovery, and grid/list controls shape the collection; a single clear action resets the active filters. Selecting a card replaces the wide catalog with a detail stage containing the canonical title, identity, status, preview host, description and source metadata, editable curator fields, review feed, and reaction form. Admins alone receive reconciliation and save controls. Counts for total, working, building, and broken exhibits remain visible above the choreography. Store methods own refreshes, selection, edits, and submitted-reaction updates. The Component performs the transition between browsing and inspection without pretending every exhibit can preview successfully.
